stories.title: The Amber-Seller's Reckoning
stories.text: In the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where glass-forgers once sang furnace-songs without fear, Korra the amber-seller kept her stall between a shuttered locksmith's shop and a kiln-house gone silent. The Dominion auditors had turned the quarter into a cage of inventory and tithe-collectors. Every spool of pre-Remaking silk, every vial of starlight-glass, every consciousness-fragment sold required a ledger-mark and a portion surrendered to the State's vaults. Korra had watched Sella the glasswright vanish into one of those vaults three months prior, caught smuggling non-compliant vessels. She'd watched bone-singers flee the city. And she'd done nothing but smile and count coins. Then a child—no more than eight, hollow-eyed, carrying a consciousness-fragment in both hands like it was water about to spill—approached her stall. "Will you trade for bread?" the girl whispered. Korra looked at the fragment: it sang of summer meadows, of a mother's name, of safety. The auditors would pay triple its weight in ash-coins. The child would starve. She made her choice in the time it took to breathe.

stories.title: The Reef-Guard's Gamble
stories.text:
Captain Merren Blackwater commanded the Greywater survey-ship from a perch above the eastern reef-walls of the Sapphire Sea, watching the boundary-waters shimmer with sickness. The Tear of the Drowned God was failing. She could taste it in the salt—an edge of abyss-rot, the smell of seals breaking after a thousand years. The deepkin council had sent her an impossible mandate: map the failing zones and recommend sacrifice-points. Five willing souls per location, offered in blood-covenant, would temporarily reinforce the seal. But the question—the one that made her grip the ship's rail until her copper hands ached—was *which five*, and *whose choice*. In the shallows below, she spotted movement: a young deepkin scout, Corvis, dragging something massive toward the reef. A wounded shadow-beast, ancient and thrashing, pulled from the abyss by the Tear's failing magic. It shouldn't exist above the seal. "All hands," Merren commanded. "Harpoons ready."

"Captain," Corvis surfaced, gasping. "Don't kill it. It's *us*—an ancestor-shade, one of the pre-human deep-dwellers. The Tear's collapse is letting them through.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Jeweled Thief
stories.text:
Kael the Nameless had been Skarath's favorite hoarder once—before the Luminous Ascension made hoarding meaningless and the wyvern-lord suddenly cared about refugee children more than gold-dunes. The transformation disgusted Kael, so she left his mountain lair and took what she could carry: a single jeweled pendant, worth a fortune in the eighth world's economics, worthless now but *beautiful*. She meant to sell it to a desperate merchant at the Wailing Scar's three-fold market. Instead, she encountered a blind cartographer named Henryk, wandering between timelines, and he offered her something stranger than payment. "That pendant," he said, "is older than empires. The jeweler who cut it was a fae-touched woman who believed that beauty itself was a form of mapping—that precious things mark the places where meaning gathered. Skarath kept it because, somewhere in his hoard-dreams, he remembered *why*."

Kael wanted to laugh. "So it's junk like everything else."

"No," Henryk said. "It's proof that value survived the Ascension. Not price—*value*. The wyvern knew it. That's why he let you keep it, even when gold became dust.

stories.title: The Bone-Flute's First Song
stories.text:
Valren was never meant to be a bone-singer. The apprentices who survived the purges were hunted now, their art classified as resistance magic by the Dominion's fragmenting ledgers. But at fifteen, hands steady and voice sure, Valren held a half-carved flute made from swan-bone and knew it was the only honest thing she'd ever touch. Naela, the elder, watched from the corner of the hidden workshop beneath the Lampwick Quarter. "Sing it first," the old woman said. "Before you finish carving. The bone remembers the song it wants to be."

Valren raised the incomplete flute and opened her throat in the Elder Tongue—syllables that predated kingdoms, that tasted like copper and starlight. The bone-dust from her carving began to dance, spiraling upward, forming patterns in the dim workshop-light. A consciousness-fragment hung suspended in one of those patterns: a child's memory of running through a garden. She felt it like a physical weight—someone else's joy, someone else's loss. And she understood, then, what the furnace-songs had done. The Dominion had taught glass-forgers to *consume* consciousness-fragments, binding them into weapon-glass.

stories.title: The Merchant's Last Bargain
stories.text: Torvin the spice-merchant had crossed the Wailing Scar so many times he'd forgotten which version of himself belonged to which timeline. In the market-that-was-three-places-at-once, he'd traded starspice for pre-Remaking silk, sold bottled starlight to transformed merchants caught between worlds, and made love to a woman named Isandra in a tavern that existed only on Thursdays. Now, on what might have been Edhran 2026-07-23, she was dying. The Luminous Ascension had caught her mid-threshold during their last transaction. Part of her existed in the eighth world, part in the ninth. Her body was forgetting how to choose. Healers said she had hours before the paradox shattered her completely. Torvin knelt beside her in a room that flickered between stone and coral. "I have something for you," he said, and drew a vial from his coat: pre-Remaking water, drawn from before the cosmos broke. It was worth more than kingdoms, more than consciousness-fragments, more than his own name. "If I drink that," Isandra whispered, "I'll anchor to one timeline. One me.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Silence
stories.text:
Karthax had hoarded for four hundred years. Gold, silver, gems—mountains of it, each coin catalogued in memory older than kingdoms. Then the Luminous Ascension shattered the world, and gold became ash. She descended from her peak in the Ironspine, claws scraping stone that no longer sang. The furnace-houses of Keshtir still burned below, but now they forged consciousness-fragments into glass weapons. The Dominion had seized control of the transformation. Karthax's hoard was worthless. Her power—built on accumulated metal—meant nothing. In the Lampwick Quarter, she found Mella, a young glass-forger hunted by auditors. The girl was hiding in a kiln, body scorched from failed furnace-songs. "You know what consciousness-fragments are," Karthax said, settling her massive frame into the workshop. Mella flinched. "Pieces of people. The Dominion bottles them, sells them as fuel."

"My hoard bought fear. Gold bought loyalty through scarcity." The wyvern's voice was ancient flint. "But fragments? They buy nothing. They only remake what was broken."

Over the following days, Karthax began to understand.

stories.title: The Reef-Keeper's Burden
stories.text: Captain Merenna's three selves were drowning. The fractured deepkin captain existed now as three autonomous echoes, each governing a third of the deepkin council's authority. Echo-Merenna maintained the pearl-beds. Mirror-Merenna commanded the reef-guard. Twin-Merenna negotiated the Tear's renewal. They were one being split, and the Tear of the Drowned God was failing faster than prophecy allowed. The boundary-waters had held for a thousand years on a covenant: five willing souls offered each season to renew the seal. But the Luminous Ascension had fractured even that. Some deepkin believed the old sacrifice was cruel; others saw mercy in refusal. The three Merennas debated with themselves in the Sapphire Deep's council chamber, their voices echoing against impossible geometries. Echo-Merenna swam to the boundary-platform where the Tear wept. The seal was cracking—not catastrophically, but visibly. Something ancient stirred in the depths below, something that remembered when the surface world was not sealed away. A young deepkin arrived: Kethiel, Echo-Merenna's own daughter. "Mother, the surface-dwellers send an emissary. A bone-singer from the Counter-Archive.

stories.title: The Thief of Rust Gate
stories.text: The Rust Gate stood at the boundary between the Civilized Reaches and the Ashen Wastes—a crumbling stone archway inscribed with syllables no auditor recognized. Beyond it lay the Bleached Wastes, where the Dominion's authority frayed into myth and sand. Before it: the tollhouse, where collectors checked every caravan for consciousness-fragments bound for the Counter-Archive. Syleth had been picking locks for refugees since the Remaking. She moved through the Ashen Reach's shadow-markets, stealing confiscated fragments from Dominion storage-vaults and delivering them to waystation librarians. It was dangerous work. Auditors had long memories and longer reach. Today's target was the Rust Gate itself. The gate did not appear on official maps. The Dominion had tried and failed to seal the threshold; it existed in a liminal space, semi-real, impossible to fully control. But the new tollhouse was very real—stone and iron, with a vault beneath it holding three centuries of confiscated pre-Remaking artifacts: compasses that sang, silk that remembered the old sun, water from a world before gold became dust.

stories.title: The Merchant's Gamble at Wailing Scar
stories.text: Hadrin had survived three Remaking timelines. At the Wailing Scar—the market that existed in three places at once—he maintained a stall where past and present collided. The Wailing Scar was not safe. Reality fractured there; the world existed as three competing versions, overlapped like imperfect reflections. A merchant could buy pre-Ascension silk from one timeline and sell it in another, profiting from the gaps between what had been and what was. But the market was destabilizing. The Dominion wanted it sealed. Bone-singers wanted it preserved. And something vast was moving beneath the surface—a pressure building toward catastrophic confluence. Hadrin sold water from the world before gold became worthless. A single vial commanded the price of a small farm. It was not greed that drove him—it was simple economy. The old water was rare. People would pay anything to touch something unchanged by the Ascension. She was a thief, half-drowned, bearing a consciousness-fragment in a sealed glass vial. "I need passage out of the Scar.

stories.title: The Druid's First Covenant
stories.text:
Elarra had lived in Thornveil Forest for forty winters, tending the ancient oaks and negotiating with the beast-spirits that dwelt beneath their roots. The Luminous Ascension had not touched the deep forest much—or so she believed, until the wounded creature stumbled into her grove. It was neither beast nor fae, neither fully alive nor entirely dream. Its form shifted: sometimes a hart with antlers of starlight, sometimes a shadow with eyes of green-growing things, sometimes something that had no name in any language the surface-world spoke. It was bleeding starlight onto moss that remembered the old sun. "What are you?" Elarra asked. "Lost," the creature whispered. "The boundary-worlds are closing. I was caught between when the Tear began to fail. If the deepkin covenant breaks entirely, I will fall into the abyss or dissolve into the upper air. There is no place for threshold-creatures when the threshold seals."

Elarra had spent four decades reading the green-growing language—the slow tongue of roots and seasons. She understood covenant.
